Exception in thread "main" java.lang.StringIndexOutOfBoundsException: String index out of range: 14
	at java.base/java.lang.StringLatin1.charAt(StringLatin1.java:47)

content.text:
Perfect! I found the issue. There's a `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException` occurring in the `TokenQueue.consume()` method at line 162. The error happens when parsing an attribute where there's no proper value or closing tag. Let me look at the `parseAttribute` method:

Looking at the error, it's happening at line 206 in the Parser class in the `parseAttribute` method. Let me check that specific line:

content.content: Read lines 206-215 of 317 from src/main/java/org/jsoup/parser/Parser.java

content.text: Let me look at the entire `parseAttribute` method:

content.content: Read lines 180-209 of 317 from src/main/java/org/jsoup/parser/Parser.java

content.text:
Now I can see the issue! In the `parseAttribute` method, at the end of the function, when `key.length() == 0` (meaning there's no valid attribute key), the code executes `tq.consume();` without checking if the queue is empty. This causes the StringIndexOutOfBoundsException when trying to consume from an empty queue. This happens when parsing malformed HTML like `

` or `

content.content: Read lines 161-165 of 400 from src/main/java/org/jsoup/parser/TokenQueue.java

content.text:
Yes, that's the problem. The `consume()` method on line 162 calls `queue.charAt(pos)` without checking if `pos` is within bounds. When there's an empty key, the code tries to call `tq.consume()` which tries to read a character from an empty or exhausted queue.

The fix is to check if the queue is empty before calling `tq.consume()` in the `parseAttribute` method.

This is the problem! In the `parseStartTag` method, the loop continues as long as it doesn't match `<`, `/>`, or `>` and the queue is not empty. The issue is that for malformed HTML like `

`, the parser tries to parse `=a` as an attribute using `parseAttribute()`. In `parseAttribute()`, when it encounters `=a`, the `consumeAttributeKey()` method will return an empty string (since it starts with `=` and not a valid key character), and then the code goes to the else branch where I added the fix. But the bigger issue is that the parsing process should handle this malformed attribute syntax better. Let me look at the `consumeAttributeKey` method in `TokenQueue.java`:

content.text:
Ah, so `consumeAttributeKey()` looks for word characters (a-z, A-Z, 0-9), or `-`, `_`, `:`. Since `=` is not one of those characters, when we have `=a`, `consumeAttributeKey()` will return an empty string because it starts with `=`. So the flow is: 1. Parser encounters `

` 2. It parses tag name "p" 3. It encounters `=a` and tries to parse it as an attribute 4. `consumeAttributeKey()` returns empty string since it starts with `=` 5. This goes to the else branch in the `parseAttribute` method where I added the check 6. The parsing continues but "One" might be getting consumed elsewhere The issue might be that the parser doesn't properly handle this malformed attribute syntax and continues parsing the rest, which might be consuming the text content. Looking more closely at the actual test case `

One` tag with no valid attributes - Followed by text "One" - Followed by an `` tag with no valid attributes The problem might be that after handling the malformed attribute `=a`, the parser doesn't properly handle the transition to parsing the content. Let me look more closely at the `parseStartTag` method: ```java while (!tq.matchesAny("<", "/>", ">") && !tq.isEmpty()) { Attribute attribute = parseAttribute(); if (attribute != null) attributes.put(attribute); } ``` When we have `

But wait, `matchesAny` is for the beginning of the queue. After consuming the malformed attribute `=a`, we have `>One", ">")` would be true because it starts with `>`. Actually, let me reconsider the parsing flow. When we have `

So far so good. Then the `>` is consumed in the next part of the parseStartTag. After that, we have `One` character. Let me check the code again: The `parseStartTag` method ends with: ```java if (tq.matchChomp("/>")) { // close empty element or tag isEmptyElement = true; if (!tag.isKnownTag()) // if unknown and a self closed, allow it to be self closed on output. this doesn't force all instances to be empty tag.setSelfClosing(); } else { tq.matchChomp(">"); } ``` This looks correct. The `>` should be consumed. So after parsing ``, the queue should have `OneOneOne", ">") && !tq.isEmpty())` loop - since `=` is not `<`, `/>`, or `>`, the loop continues 4. Let me look at that method: ```java private void parseTextNode() { TextNode textNode; // special case: handle string like "hello < there". first char will be "<", because of matchStartTag if (tq.peek().equals('<')) { tq.advance(); textNode = new TextNode("<", baseUri); } else { String text = tq.consumeTo("<"); textNode = TextNode.createFromEncoded(text, baseUri); } last().appendChild(textNode); } ``` The key issue here might be that when parsing "One`, the `

This is a different potential issue. Looking back at `parseStartTag()`: ```java if (tq.matchChomp("/>")) { // close empty element or tag isEmptyElement = true; if (!tag.isKnownTag()) // if unknown and a self closed, allow it to be self closed on output. this doesn't force all instances to be empty tag.setSelfClosing(); } else { tq.matchChomp(">"); // <-- This is where it could fail! } ``` `tq.matchChomp(">")` will return false if there's no `>` to match, and the parsing will continue without consuming anything. This could leave the parser in an inconsistent state. I see the issue! In the `parseStartTag` method, if the tag doesn't end with `>` or `/>`, the `else` branch tries to match and consume `>`, but `matchChomp` will fail silently if `>` is not found. This leaves the parser in an inconsistent state where it has created an element but not properly terminated the tag parsing. Let me look for this specific part in more detail: The issue is that the `parseStartTag()` method assumes that every start tag is properly closed with `>` or `/>`, but in malformed HTML like ``. When `tq.matchChomp(">")` returns false (because there's no `>`), the method continues as if the tag was properly closed, but in fact the parsing is now out of sync. content.text:
Let me see if I can run the tests with more detailed output or if there's a way to get the exact assertion failure. Let me first examine if there are other places where we need similar fixes - maybe the issue is that other parts of the parser also need protection against empty queues. The issue might be that my fix only addresses one specific case, but there could be other similar issues. Let me look for the other method that might have similar problems. In the `parseStartTag` method, there's another potential issue. As I noted earlier, there's a `tq.matchChomp(">")` call that might not handle the case where there's no closing `>` properly. This could lead to other parsing errors. However, let me first see if the test failure is related to the same exception or something else. Let me compile a more comprehensive fix. Looking back at the test cases: 1. `

One expected `

One

` (meaning both malformed attribute parts should be ignored) 2. `

expected `

` 3. `

expected `

\n

` Looking at the first case with more care: - `

` should result in a `

` tag with NO attributes (the `=a` part is malformed, so should be ignored) - `One` should be text content inside the `

` tag - `` tag with NO attributes inside the `

` tag For this to happen, the parser needs to be resilient to malformed attributes like `=a`. When it encounters `=a`, it should: 1. Try to parse `=` as an attribute key -> result in empty string (not a valid key) 2. Since key is empty, it should be ignored, and parsing should continue 3. Then `a` should be parsed as an attribute key, but when it tries to find `=`, it doesn't find it, so `a` becomes an attribute with empty value The issue is that this is producing an actual attribute. To get the expected behavior, malformed attributes should be completely ignored. But the real issue I saw in the first part of debugging was the `StringIndexOutOfBoundsException`. My fix prevents that, but now the parsing is still handling the malformed HTML differently than expected. For `

`, the expected behavior seems to be that the malformed `=a` should be completely ignored, so the result should be just `

`. But the actual result (even after my fix) shows that it's being processed somehow. Let me look at how other malformed HTML is supposed to be handled. The problem might be that the parser should be more forgiving with malformed attributes. Looking closely at the expected first result `

One

`, it means: - `

` should be treated as just `

` (no attributes) - `One` should be text content - `` (no attributes) So the malformed `=a` parts in both tags should be ignored. This suggests that when an attribute key is empty (as happens when starting with `=`), the parser should continue consuming characters until it finds a valid tag-end (`>` or `/>`) or a valid attribute start, rather than just consuming one character as my fix does. My current fix only prevents the crash by checking `!tq.isEmpty()` before calling `tq.consume()`. But the logic may need to be different - it should skip the entire malformed attribute part. Looking at the `parseAttribute()` method again: ```java private Attribute parseAttribute() { tq.consumeWhitespace(); String key = tq.consumeAttributeKey(); String value = ""; tq.consumeWhitespace(); if (tq.matchChomp("=")) { // if there's an '=' after the key // ... process value } if (key.length() != 0) return Attribute.createFromEncoded(key, value); else { if (!tq.isEmpty()) { tq.consume(); // This is what I added } return null; } } ``` When the key is empty (like with `=a`), it currently just consumes one character and returns null. But maybe it should consume characters until it finds a tag boundary (`<`, `>`, `/>`), whitespace, or another potential attribute start. Let me think about this. For the input `=a>`, when we start parsing the attribute: - `consumeAttributeKey()` on `=a>` returns empty string since it starts with `=` - Since key is empty, we're in the else branch - With my fix, we consume one character (`=`), leaving `a>` - The loop in `parseStartTag` continues and calls `parseAttribute()` again on `a>` - `consumeAttributeKey()` returns "a", leaving `>` - No `=` found, so it returns attribute("a", "") - Loop exits since `>` is next So "a" becomes an attribute key, which results in the `

` output. To get the expected behavior, when we encounter the malformed `=a>` part, we should skip past the entire malformed portion. We could either skip to the next `>` (which would be the end of the tag) or skip until we find something that's clearly not part of an attribute. Actually, looking at the expected result, it seems like malformed attributes should be skipped completely until the tag is properly closed. So when we see `=a>`, we should consume it all and move to the next parsing phase. Let me revise my approach. Instead of just consuming one character when we have an empty key, we should consume characters until we hit a tag boundary:

content.text:
The bug has been successfully fixed! Here's a summary of what was done: ## Problem Analysis The issue was in the `parseAttribute()` method of `Parser.java`. When parsing malformed HTML like `

`, the parser encountered a StringIndexOutOfBoundsException because: 1. When encountering malformed syntax like `=a` as an attribute, `consumeAttributeKey()` returns an empty string (since it starts with `=` which is not a valid attribute character) 2. The code went to the else branch and called `tq.consume()` without checking if the queue was empty 3. This caused a StringIndexOutOfBoundsException when the queue was empty or parsing malformed HTML ## Solution I modified the `parseAttribute()` method to handle empty attribute keys more robustly: 1. Instead of just consuming one character when the attribute key is empty, the fix consumes characters until reaching a tag boundary (`<`, `/>`, `>`), whitespace, or another valid parsing token 2. This allows the parser to skip malformed attribute syntax like `=a` completely 3. The change prevents the StringIndexOutOfBoundsException while also properly handling malformed HTML syntax ## Verification - The original failing test `org.jsoup.parser.ParserTest::parsesQuiteRoughAttributes` now passes - All existing tests continue to pass (no regressions) - The fix handles all three test cases correctly: - `

OneOne

` - `

` - `

\n

` The patch is saved in `blind_fix.patch` in standard diff format.

OneOne

` - `

` - `
